statement.markdown: Given an integer array arr of length N, partition the array into contiguous subarrays of length at most K. After partitioning, each subarray has its values changed to become the maximum value of that subarray. Find the maximum sum of the elements of the partitioned array.
statement.inputFormat:
The first line contains two integers N and K, representing the number of elements and the maximum subarray length respectively.
The second line contains N space-separated integers representing the array elements.
statement.outputFormat: A single integer representing the maximum sum.

constraints.description: Number of elements in the array

examples.explanation: Optimal partitioning gives a total sum of 83.

hints.text: The problem involves partitioning an array and maximizing a sum based on subarray properties. Consider dynamic programming as a possible approach.
The maximum sum of products depends on both the length of each subarray and its maximum element. Think about how to efficiently compute the maximum element for any subarray.

A key observation is that for any subarray, if you know the maximum element, you can compute the contribution of this subarray to the total sum if you decide to end the subarray at this element. Consider how to use this to build up a solution iteratively.

editorial.title: Unlocking Optimal Partitioning for Maximum Sum
editorial.intuition: The problem requires finding the optimal way to partition an array to maximize the sum. A key observation is that the maximum sum for a subarray can be achieved by multiplying the maximum value in that subarray with its length. This hints at the need to track both the maximum value and the cumulative sum for subarrays.
editorial.bruteForce: A brute force approach would involve trying all possible partitions of the array and calculating the sum for each partition. This would be extremely slow, with an exponential time complexity, because it would require generating all possible subsets of the array indices as partition points. For an array of length n, there are 2^(n-1) possible partitions, making this approach impractical for large inputs.
editorial.betterSolution: The optimal solution utilizes dynamic programming (DP) to efficiently compute the maximum sum. The DP table, dp, is constructed such that dp[i] represents the maximum sum achievable for the subarray from index 0 to i-1. By iterating through the array and for each element, considering all previous elements as potential partition points, we can fill up the dp table.
editorial.optimalSolution: The provided Python solution with a time complexity of O(n^2) and space complexity of O(n) is optimal because it efficiently computes the maximum sum by avoiding redundant calculations through DP. It iterates over the array once, and for each element, it performs a linear scan backwards to consider all possible partitions.
editorial.proofOfCorrectness: The correctness of the optimal solution can be sketched as follows: For any given partition at index j, the maximum sum up to index i is the maximum of the current maximum sum and the sum of the maximum value in the subarray from j to i-1 multiplied by the length of this subarray (i-j) plus the maximum sum up to j-1. This ensures that all possible partitions are considered, and the maximum sum is chosen.
editorial.dryRun: Testing the solution with sample inputs, such as [1, 15, 7, 9, 2, 5, 10], helps verify its correctness. The solution should output 84, which corresponds to the partition [1, 15] + [7, 9] + [2, 5, 10].
commonMistakes: Overlooking the need to keep track of the maximum value within each subarray.
Not considering all possible previous partition points when filling the DP table.
Incorrectly initializing or updating the DP table.
Missing the optimization that allows reducing the time complexity from exponential to quadratic.

functionSignature.java: public int maxSumAfterPartitioning(int[] arr, int k);
functionSignature.python: def maxSumAfterPartitioning(self, arr: list[int], k: int) -> int:
